meta.title: PostgreSQL
meta.description: Review checklist for PostgreSQL migrations, schema design, and queries

items.text: Is the migration reversible (has a corresponding down/rollback)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every migration should have a rollback strategy — either a down migration or a documented manual rollback plan.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a deployment goes wrong, you need to roll back quickly. Irreversible migrations force you to write a new forward migration under pressure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if a down migration exists. Does it correctly undo the up migration? Has the rollback been tested?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column and backfills data but has no down migration. Add one that removes the column.

codeExamples.label: Reversible

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every up migration needs a down migration. Test both directions.

items.text: Can this migration run without downtime (no exclusive locks on hot tables)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations that take exclusive locks on heavily-used tables cause downtime. ALTER TABLE operations vary in lock requirements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An ALTER TABLE on a table with millions of rows can lock it for minutes, blocking all reads and writes and causing an outage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for ALTER TABLE on large tables. Adding a column with a default (pre-PG11) requires a full table rewrite. Adding NOT NULL without a default requires a full scan.
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a NOT NULL constraint to a table with 50M rows. Use ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID followed by VALIDATE CONSTRAINT to avoid a full table lock.

codeExamples.label: Blocking
codeExamples.code: ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending';

codeExamples.label: Non-blocking (PG 11+)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test migrations against production-sized data. What takes 1ms on dev can take 10 minutes on prod.

items.text: Is the migration wrapped in a transaction (or explicitly opted out)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: DDL in PostgreSQL is transactional — migrations should use transactions so partial failures don't leave the schema in a broken state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A migration that fails halfway without a transaction leaves the database in an inconsistent state that's painful to fix manually.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the migration tool wrap DDL in a transaction? If not, is there a BEGIN/COMMIT? Are there operations that can't run in a transaction (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration creates a table and then creates an index. If the index creation fails, the table exists but the index doesn't. Wrap in a transaction.

codeExamples.label: Note
codeExamples.code:
-- CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction.
-- Split it into a separate migration.

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use transactions for DDL. Split out operations that can't run in transactions (CONCURRENTLY, ENUM values).

items.text: Are schema changes and data migrations in separate steps?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Schema migrations (DDL) and data migrations (DML) should be separate. Mixing them makes rollbacks harder and increases lock time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A migration that alters a table AND backfills data holds locks longer and is harder to roll back if the backfill fails.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the migration mix CREATE/ALTER with UPDATE/INSERT? Can the data migration run as a separate step?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column AND backfills 10M rows in one step. Split into: 1) add column, 2) backfill in batches, 3) add NOT NULL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Separate DDL from DML. Deploy schema first, then migrate data in batches.

items.text: Are indexes on large tables created with CONCURRENTLY?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CREATE INDEX locks the table for writes during the build. CONCURRENTLY builds the index without blocking writes (but takes longer).
baseContent.whyItMatters: A regular CREATE INDEX on a 100M-row table can block writes for minutes, causing application errors and timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is CREATE INDEX used on a large table? Should it be CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY? Note: CONCURRENTLY can't run in a transaction.
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates an index on a 50M-row table without CONCURRENTLY. This will block all writes for several minutes. Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

codeExamples.label: Non-blocking
codeExamples.code: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_user_id ON orders (user_id);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use CONCURRENTLY for indexes on large production tables.

items.text: Are new enum values added safely (not inside a transaction if using CONCURRENTLY)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Adding enum values in PostgreSQL is safe but has quirks: ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE cannot run inside a transaction block in some versions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If your migration tool wraps everything in a transaction, ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE will fail. It needs its own non-transactional migration.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is a new enum value being added? Is the migration tool configured to run it outside a transaction?
baseContent.exampleComment: Split the ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE into its own migration file with transaction disabled, since it can't run inside a transaction.

codeExamples.label: Add enum value
codeExamples.code:
-- Must be outside a transaction in some migration tools
ALTER TYPE order_status ADD VALUE 'refunded';

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enum additions need their own migration without a transaction wrapper.

items.text: Do table and column names follow consistent naming conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PostgreSQL conventions: lowercase snake_case for tables and columns, plural table names (users, orders), singular for join tables.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming makes queries harder to write and the schema harder to navigate. It's a constant source of friction.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are table names plural and snake_case? Are column names descriptive? Are foreign keys named consistently (user_id, not userId or fk_user)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The table is named `OrderItem` (PascalCase) while others use `order_items` (snake_case). Use snake_case for consistency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use lowercase snake_case everywhere. Be consistent — it matters more than which convention you pick.

items.text: Are columns using the most appropriate PostgreSQL data types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PostgreSQL has rich type support. Using TEXT for everything wastes the benefits of type safety, indexing, and storage efficiency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong types waste storage, prevent useful constraints, hurt query performance, and allow invalid data. Using VARCHAR(255) everywhere is a MySQL habit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is TEXT used where an enum, integer, or timestamp would be better? Is VARCHAR(255) used without a reason? Are UUIDs stored as TEXT instead of UUID?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `status` column is TEXT but only has 5 valid values. Use a PostgreSQL enum or a CHECK constraint for type safety.

codeExamples.label: Better types

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use PostgreSQL's rich type system. UUID for IDs, TIMESTAMPTZ for times, enums for fixed sets, INTEGER for counts.

items.text: Are timestamp columns using TIMESTAMPTZ (not TIMESTAMP)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TIMESTAMPTZ stores timestamps in UTC and converts to the session timezone on read. TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE stores ambiguous local times.
baseContent.whyItMatters: TIMESTAMP without timezone causes bugs when servers are in different timezones, during DST transitions, and when migrating data between systems.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all timestamp columns. Are they TIMESTAMPTZ? The only exception is when you intentionally need a timezone-unaware local time.
baseContent.exampleComment: Change `created_at TIMESTAMP` to `created_at TIMESTAMPTZ`. Without timezone info, this timestamp is ambiguous across timezones.

codeExamples.label: Correct
codeExamples.code: created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use TIMESTAMPTZ. The "without timezone" variant is almost never what you want.

items.text: Does every table have an appropriate primary key?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every table should have a primary key. It enables efficient lookups, is required for replication, and prevents duplicate rows.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tables without primary keys can't be efficiently replicated, can accumulate duplicate rows, and make certain queries very slow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new table have a PRIMARY KEY? Is the key type appropriate (UUID vs SERIAL vs natural key)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This table has no primary key. Add `id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()` or use an appropriate natural key.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every table needs a primary key. Prefer UUIDs for distributed systems, BIGSERIAL for simple apps.

items.text: Are foreign key relationships properly defined?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Foreign keys enforce referential integrity — they prevent orphaned rows and make relationships explicit in the schema.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without foreign keys, orphaned data accumulates silently. Deleting a user leaves their orders pointing at nothing, causing application errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are relationships between tables enforced with FOREIGN KEY constraints? Is ON DELETE behavior specified (CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `orders.user_id` column references `users.id` but has no FOREIGN KEY constraint. Add one with ON DELETE CASCADE.

codeExamples.label: With FK
codeExamples.code:
ALTER TABLE orders
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_user
  FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
  ON DELETE CASCADE;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define foreign keys for all relationships. Choose ON DELETE behavior intentionally.

items.text: Are NOT NULL constraints applied where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Columns that should always have values need NOT NULL constraints. Nullable columns should be intentionally nullable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing NOT NULL allows null values where they don't make sense, leading to unexpected NullPointerExceptions and broken aggregations.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each new column, should it allow nulls? Is there a meaningful default? Are required fields marked NOT NULL?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `email` column allows NULL but every user must have an email. Add NOT NULL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to NOT NULL. Make columns nullable only when null has a clear meaning.

items.text: Are there indexes supporting the application's query patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Queries that filter, join, or order by columns need indexes on those columns. Without them, PostgreSQL does full table scans.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A missing index on a frequently-queried column causes O(n) sequential scans instead of O(log n) index lookups. This gets worse as data grows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the application's queries against this table. Do WHERE clauses and JOIN conditions have supporting indexes?
baseContent.exampleComment: The app queries `SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = ? AND status = ?` but there's no index on `(user_id, status)`. Add a composite index.

codeExamples.label: Composite index
codeExamples.code: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_status ON orders (user_id, status);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Index the columns you query by. Use EXPLAIN to verify your queries use them.

items.text: Could a covering index (INCLUDE) avoid table lookups?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A covering index includes all columns needed by a query, allowing index-only scans without reading the table heap.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Index-only scans are significantly faster because they skip the random I/O of reading table pages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does a frequently-run query select columns not in the index? Would adding INCLUDE columns enable index-only scans?
baseContent.exampleComment: This query always selects `user_id, status, total`. Adding `INCLUDE (total)` to the `(user_id, status)` index enables index-only scans.

codeExamples.label: Covering index

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use INCLUDE for columns that are selected but not filtered on. Check with EXPLAIN for index-only scans.

items.text: Could a partial index reduce index size for filtered queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Partial indexes only index rows matching a WHERE condition. They're smaller and faster when queries always filter on that condition.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A full index on a status column where 95% of rows are 'completed' wastes space. A partial index on active rows is much smaller.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the application always query with a specific filter (e.g., WHERE active = true, WHERE deleted_at IS NULL)? A partial index would help.
baseContent.exampleComment: Since queries always filter `WHERE status = 'active'`, use a partial index: `CREATE INDEX ... WHERE status = 'active'`.

codeExamples.label: Partial index

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Partial indexes are smaller and faster. Use them when queries always include the same filter condition.

items.text: Is the column order in composite indexes optimal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In composite indexes, column order matters. The leftmost columns must match the query's filter/sort pattern for the index to be used.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An index on (status, user_id) won't be used for `WHERE user_id = ?` queries. Column order must match query patterns.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check query patterns. Does the index column order match the most selective filters first? Can one composite index serve multiple queries?
baseContent.exampleComment: The index is `(status, user_id)` but most queries filter on `user_id` only. Reverse the order to `(user_id, status)`.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Put the most selective and most frequently filtered column first in composite indexes.

items.text: Are there duplicate or redundant indexes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Duplicate indexes waste storage, slow down writes, and confuse the query planner. An index on (a, b) makes a standalone index on (a) redundant.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each index adds overhead to every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Redundant indexes double this cost with no benefit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for indexes with the same leading columns. Is there both an index on (user_id) and (user_id, status)?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's already an index on `(user_id, status)` which covers queries on `user_id` alone. The standalone `user_id` index is redundant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Audit indexes periodically. A composite index on (a, b) covers queries on (a).

items.text: Does the query pattern avoid N+1 queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: N+1 queries happen when code fetches a list, then makes one query per item to fetch related data. This causes O(n) queries instead of O(1).
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the most common performance problem. 100 orders with N+1 means 101 queries instead of 1-2 with a JOIN or IN clause.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the code fetch a list then loop to fetch related data? Could a JOIN, subquery, or IN clause combine these?
baseContent.exampleComment: This fetches all orders, then queries the user for each order in a loop. Use a JOIN: `SELECT o.*, u.name FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id`.

codeExamples.label: N+1 problem
codeExamples.code:
-- 1 query for orders
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'active';
-- Then N queries, one per order
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1;  -- repeated N times

codeExamples.label: Fixed with JOIN
codeExamples.code:
SELECT o.*, u.name, u.email
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.status = 'active';

baseContent.keyTakeaway: JOINs and IN clauses replace N+1 queries. Always batch related data fetches.

items.text: Has EXPLAIN ANALYZE been run on new or modified queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows the actual query plan, execution time, and row estimates. It reveals sequential scans, bad estimates, and missing indexes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A query that's fast on dev with 100 rows can be catastrophically slow on production with 10M rows. EXPLAIN catches this early.
baseContent.howToVerify: Has the developer run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the new query with production-representative data? Are there sequential scans on large tables?
baseContent.exampleComment: Please include EXPLAIN ANALYZE output for this new query. It joins 3 tables and I want to verify it uses indexes properly.

codeExamples.label: Running EXPLAIN
codeExamples.code:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE user_id = '...' AND status = 'active'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on every new query before deploying. It's the single best way to catch performance issues.

items.text: Is pagination implemented efficiently (keyset, not OFFSET)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: OFFSET-based pagination gets slower as pages increase because the database must scan and discard all preceding rows. Keyset pagination stays constant.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Page 1000 with OFFSET 10000 requires scanning 10000 rows just to discard them. Keyset pagination scans only the rows it returns.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the query use OFFSET for pagination? Could it use keyset pagination (WHERE id > last_seen_id) instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This query uses `OFFSET $page * 20` which gets slower on later pages. Use keyset pagination: `WHERE created_at < $last_created_at ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20`.

codeExamples.label: Keyset pagination
codeExamples.code:
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE user_id = $1
  AND created_at < $2  -- cursor from previous page
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use keyset (cursor) pagination for any list that could be large. OFFSET is only acceptable for small datasets.

items.text: Are SELECT * queries avoided in production code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SELECT * fetches all columns, including large TEXT/JSONB fields that may not be needed. It also breaks when columns are added.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fetching unnecessary columns wastes bandwidth, memory, and can prevent index-only scans. It also couples code to the table schema.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the query use SELECT *? Would listing specific columns improve performance or clarity?
baseContent.exampleComment: This query uses `SELECT *` but only needs `id`, `title`, and `status`. The `notes` column is a large TEXT field that's wasted here.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: List the columns you need. SELECT * is for debugging, not production queries.

items.text: Do write queries minimize lock contention?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Long-running transactions and batch updates can hold row locks for extended periods, blocking other queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A batch UPDATE that locks 10000 rows for 30 seconds blocks all other writes to those rows, causing timeouts and queue buildup.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are batch operations chunked? Are transactions kept short? Do updates touch only necessary rows?
baseContent.exampleComment: This batch update processes 100K rows in one transaction. Chunk it into batches of 1000 with a brief pause between batches.

codeExamples.label: Batched update
codeExamples.code:
-- Process in chunks
UPDATE orders SET status = 'archived'
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT id FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
    AND completed_at < now() - interval '1 year'
  LIMIT 1000
  FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep transactions short. Chunk batch operations. Use SKIP LOCKED for queue-like patterns.

items.text: Are all queries parameterized (no string concatenation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SQL queries must use parameterized queries ($1, $2) or prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection is one of the most critical vulnerabilities. An attacker can read, modify, or delete all data in the database.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all query construction. Is user input concatenated into SQL strings? Are query builders used safely?
baseContent.exampleComment: This query concatenates `username` directly into the SQL string. Use a parameterized query: `WHERE username = $1`.

codeExamples.label: Vulnerable
codeExamples.code: cursor.execute(f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{name}'")

codeExamples.label: Safe
codeExamples.code: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = %s", (name,))

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always parameterize. There are no exceptions. If you think you need dynamic SQL, use a query builder.

items.text: Does the application use a database role with minimal privileges?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The application's database role should have only the permissions it needs — typically SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on specific tables.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An app role with SUPERUSER or CREATEDB permissions means a SQL injection attack can drop databases or read system files.
baseContent.howToVerify: What role does the app connect with? Does it have more permissions than needed? Can it DROP tables or access system catalogs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The application connects as the postgres superuser. Create a dedicated role with only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on application tables.

codeExamples.label: Least privilege

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use superuser for application connections. Create a role with minimal permissions.

items.text: Is row-level security considered for multi-tenant data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) enforces data isolation at the database level, preventing one tenant from seeing another's data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Application-level tenant isolation relies on every query including the correct WHERE clause. One missed filter leaks data across tenants.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is this a multi-tenant application? Are WHERE clauses the only thing preventing cross-tenant data access? Would RLS add a safety net?
baseContent.exampleComment: This multi-tenant app relies on `WHERE tenant_id = $1` in every query. Consider adding RLS as a defense-in-depth measure.

codeExamples.label: RLS policy

baseContent.keyTakeaway: RLS is defense-in-depth for multi-tenant apps. It catches the queries where you forgot the tenant filter.

items.text: Is sensitive data encrypted at rest or properly handled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sensitive data (PII, credentials, tokens) should be encrypted in the database or handled with appropriate access controls.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unencrypted sensitive data is exposed in backups, replicas, logs, and to anyone with database read access.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there columns containing PII, passwords, or tokens? Are they encrypted with pgcrypto or application-level encryption?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `social_security_number` column is stored as plain TEXT. Encrypt it with pgcrypto or application-level encryption.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encrypt sensitive data. Use pgcrypto for database-level encryption or handle it in the application layer.

items.text: Is the transaction isolation level appropriate for the operation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PostgreSQL supports READ COMMITTED (default), REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE. Each has different consistency guarantees and performance characteristics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The wrong isolation level can cause phantom reads, lost updates, or unnecessary serialization failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the transaction require stronger isolation than READ COMMITTED? Would SERIALIZABLE prevent the race condition being guarded against?
baseContent.exampleComment: This read-then-write pattern has a race condition under READ COMMITTED. Either use SERIALIZABLE or add explicit row locking with SELECT FOR UPDATE.

codeExamples.label: Explicit locking

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Know your isolation levels. Use SELECT FOR UPDATE for read-then-write patterns.

items.text: Is there a risk of deadlocks with this transaction pattern?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deadlocks occur when two transactions lock resources in different orders. PostgreSQL detects and resolves them by aborting one transaction.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Deadlocks cause transaction failures that must be retried. Frequent deadlocks indicate a design problem that degrades performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this transaction lock multiple rows or tables? Could another transaction lock the same resources in a different order?
baseContent.exampleComment: This transaction updates orders then users, but another code path updates users then orders. Standardize the lock order to prevent deadlocks.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lock resources in a consistent order across all code paths. Document the expected lock order.

items.text: Are transactions kept as short as possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Long-running transactions hold locks, prevent vacuum from cleaning up dead tuples, and increase the risk of conflicts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A transaction open for minutes causes table bloat (dead tuples), increases lock contention, and can cause replication lag.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the transaction do any slow operations (HTTP calls, file I/O) while holding locks? Can it be shortened?
baseContent.exampleComment: This transaction makes an HTTP call to an external API while holding a row lock. Move the API call outside the transaction.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Do I/O outside transactions. Keep transactions to the minimum: read, compute, write, commit.

items.text: Are serialization failures handled with retries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SERIALIZABLE and REPEATABLE READ transactions can fail with serialization errors. The application must retry these transparently.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without retry logic, serialization failures surface as user-visible errors even though they're expected and transient.
baseContent.howToVerify: If using SERIALIZABLE isolation, does the code retry on error code 40001 (serialization failure)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This code uses SERIALIZABLE but doesn't retry on serialization failures. Add a retry loop for error code 40001.

codeExamples.label: Retry pattern

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always retry serialization failures. They're expected, not exceptional.

items.text: Are CHECK constraints used to enforce business rules?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CHECK constraints enforce domain rules at the database level (e.g., price > 0, status IN ('active', 'inactive')).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Application-level validation can be bypassed by direct SQL, migrations, or other services. Database constraints are the last line of defense.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there business rules that should be enforced at the database level? Are numeric ranges validated? Are string values constrained?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `price` column allows negative values. Add a CHECK constraint: `CHECK (price >= 0)`.

codeExamples.label: CHECK constraint
codeExamples.code:
ALTER TABLE products
  ADD CONSTRAINT chk_price_positive CHECK (price >= 0);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Database constraints survive code changes, migrations, and direct SQL. Use them for critical business rules.

items.text: Are unique constraints defined where duplicates would be invalid?

baseContent.whatItMeans: UNIQUE constraints prevent duplicate values. They're essential for email addresses, usernames, natural keys, and combination uniqueness.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without unique constraints, duplicate data can slip in through race conditions, bulk imports, or bugs — corrupting the dataset.
baseContent.howToVerify: Should any column or combination of columns be unique? Is there a UNIQUE constraint or unique index?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `email` column should be unique but has no UNIQUE constraint. Two users can register with the same email.

codeExamples.label: Unique constraint
codeExamples.code: ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT uq_users_email UNIQUE (email);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If duplicates would be a bug, add a UNIQUE constraint. Don't rely on application code alone.

items.text: Is ON DELETE/UPDATE behavior explicitly specified for foreign keys?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Foreign keys should explicitly state what happens on delete/update: CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT, or NO ACTION.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The default (NO ACTION) blocks deletion of referenced rows, which may not be the intended behavior. Explicit declaration prevents surprises.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do foreign keys specify ON DELETE behavior? Is CASCADE used intentionally (not accidentally deleting important data)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This foreign key uses the default NO ACTION. When a user is deleted, their orders become orphaned. Use CASCADE or SET NULL intentionally.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be explicit about ON DELETE behavior. Document why CASCADE vs SET NULL was chosen.

items.text: Are DEFAULT values appropriate and intentional?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Default values should be intentional. Defaulting to empty string instead of NULL, or 0 instead of NULL, can mask missing data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bad defaults hide missing data. A default of 0 for a price field means you can't distinguish 'free' from 'not yet set'.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are DEFAULT values appropriate? Would NULL be better for optional fields? Is `now()` used for timestamps?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `deleted_at` column defaults to `'1970-01-01'` instead of NULL. Use NULL to indicate 'not deleted'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use NULL for 'not set'. Use meaningful defaults only when there's a sensible value.

items.text: Has the impact on autovacuum been considered for bulk operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Bulk updates and deletes create dead tuples that autovacuum must clean up. Large batch operations can overwhelm autovacuum.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If autovacuum can't keep up, table bloat increases, queries slow down, and eventually transaction ID wraparound becomes a risk.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does this migration or batch job update/delete many rows? Will autovacuum be able to keep up? Should vacuum be run manually after?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration updates 10M rows. Schedule a manual VACUUM ANALYZE on the table after the migration completes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor autovacuum after large data changes. Run VACUUM ANALYZE manually for bulk operations.

items.text: Is connection pooling configured for the application?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PostgreSQL has a per-connection process model. Each connection uses ~10MB RAM. Without pooling, connection storms can exhaust server resources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 100 application instances each opening 10 connections = 1000 connections = 10GB RAM just for connections. PgBouncer or application pooling is essential.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the application using a connection pool? What's the max pool size? Is PgBouncer or pgpool used for production?
baseContent.exampleComment: Each Lambda invocation opens a new database connection. Use RDS Proxy or PgBouncer to pool connections.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use connection pooling in production. Size pools based on available connections.

items.text: Are there monitoring queries or alerts for the new tables?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New tables should be added to monitoring: table size, row counts, dead tuple ratios, and query performance.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tables that aren't monitored silently grow, accumulate bloat, or develop performance issues that go unnoticed until they cause outages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the new table added to monitoring dashboards? Are there alerts for table size thresholds or slow queries?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add this new table to the pg_stat_user_tables monitoring dashboard so we can track its growth and vacuum health.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor what you deploy. Add new tables to dashboards and alert rules.

items.text: Has backup and restore been tested for the new schema?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Schema changes should be tested against backup/restore procedures to ensure backups are still restorable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A schema change that breaks restore means your backups are useless when you need them most.
baseContent.howToVerify: Has pg_dump/pg_restore been tested with the new schema? Are there new tables that need to be included in selective backups?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new `tracked_prs` table should be included in the backup configuration. Verify pg_restore works with the updated schema.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test backup and restore after schema changes. Untested backups are not backups.
